content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Devoicing}} is defined as: /i/ and /u/ are often devoiced when between two voiceless consonants or at the end of a phrase (e.g., *desu* [desɯ̥]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Vowel Devoicing' es: /i/ and /u/ are often devoiced when between two voiceless consonants or at the end of a phrase (e.g., *desu* [desɯ̥]).
content.usage:
Rule: Vowel Devoicing
Description: /i/ and /u/ are often devoiced when between two voiceless consonants or at the end of a phrase (e.g., *desu* [desɯ̥]).

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Length}} is defined as: Japanese distinguishes between short and long vowels (e.g., *ojisan* "uncle" vs *ojiisan* "grandfather").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Vowel Length' es: Japanese distinguishes between short and long vowels (e.g., *ojisan* "uncle" vs *ojiisan* "grandfather").
content.usage:
Rule: Vowel Length
Description: Japanese distinguishes between short and long vowels (e.g., *ojisan* "uncle" vs *ojiisan* "grandfather").

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::No Diphthongs}} is defined as: Sequences of vowels are usually treated as separate moras.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'No Diphthongs' es: Sequences of vowels are usually treated as separate moras.
content.usage:
Rule: No Diphthongs
Description: Sequences of vowels are usually treated as separate moras.

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Moraic Nasal (ん)}} is defined as: /ɴ/ changes its pronunciation depending on the following sound (e.g., [m] before /p, b, m/, [ŋ] before /k, ɡ/).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'The Moraic Nasal (ん)' es: /ɴ/ changes its pronunciation depending on the following sound (e.g., [m] before /p, b, m/, [ŋ] before /k, ɡ/).
content.usage:
Rule: The Moraic Nasal (ん)
Description: /ɴ/ changes its pronunciation depending on the following sound (e.g., [m] before /p, b, m/, [ŋ] before /k, ɡ/).

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Japanese 'R'}} is defined as: An alveolar tap /ɾ/, somewhere between an English 'd' and 'l'.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'The Japanese 'R'' es: An alveolar tap /ɾ/, somewhere between an English 'd' and 'l'.
content.usage:
Rule: The Japanese 'R'
Description: An alveolar tap /ɾ/, somewhere between an English 'd' and 'l'.

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Gemination (っ)}} is defined as: Double consonants (e.g., *matte*) count as a full mora and involve a distinct pause or holding of the consonant position.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Gemination (っ)' es: Double consonants (e.g., *matte*) count as a full mora and involve a distinct pause or holding of the consonant position.
content.usage:
Rule: Gemination (っ)
Description: Double consonants (e.g., *matte*) count as a full mora and involve a distinct pause or holding of the consonant position.

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Mora-timed Rhythm}} is defined as: Keep a steady tempo where each sound unit (mora), including the double consonant っ (Q) and the nasal ん (N), receives exactly one beat of time (e.g., *kitte* [ki.t.te] has 3 moras; *shinkansen* has 5).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Mora-timed Rhythm' es: Keep a steady tempo where each sound unit (mora), including the double consonant っ (Q) and the nasal ん (N), receives exactly one beat of time (e.g., *kitte* [ki.t.te] has 3 moras; *shinkansen* has 5).
content.usage:
Rule: Mora-timed Rhythm
Description: Keep a steady tempo where each sound unit (mora), including the double consonant っ (Q) and the nasal ん (N), receives exactly one beat of time (e.g., *kitte* [ki.t.te] has 3 moras; *shinkansen* has 5).

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Devoicing}} is defined as: Vowels /i/ and /u/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants (/p, t, k, s, h, ch, sh/) and at the end of statements after a voiceless consonant (e.g., *desu* -> [desɯ̥], *sukiyaki* -> [sɯ̥kijaki], *shitsurei* -> [ɕi̥tsɯɾeː]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Vowel Devoicing' es: Vowels /i/ and /u/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants (/p, t, k, s, h, ch, sh/) and at the end of statements after a voiceless consonant (e.g., *desu* -> [desɯ̥], *sukiyaki* -> [sɯ̥kijaki], *shitsurei* -> [ɕi̥tsɯɾeː]).
content.usage:
Rule: Vowel Devoicing
Description: Vowels /i/ and /u/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants (/p, t, k, s, h, ch, sh/) and at the end of statements after a voiceless consonant (e.g., *desu* -> [desɯ̥], *sukiyaki* -> [sɯ̥kijaki], *shitsurei* -> [ɕi̥tsɯɾeː]).

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::~de shimau}} is defined as: > **~jau** (e.g., *ninde shimau* -> "ninjau").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de '~de shimau' es: > **~jau** (e.g., *ninde shimau* -> "ninjau").
content.usage:
Rule: ~de shimau
Description: > **~jau** (e.g., *ninde shimau* -> "ninjau").

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::~te wa}} is defined as: > **~cha** (e.g., *tabete wa ikenai* -> "tabecha ikenai").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de '~te wa' es: > **~cha** (e.g., *tabete wa ikenai* -> "tabecha ikenai").
content.usage:
Rule: ~te wa
Description: > **~cha** (e.g., *tabete wa ikenai* -> "tabecha ikenai").

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::~de wa}} is defined as: > **~ja** (e.g., *dewa mata* -> "ja mata").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de '~de wa' es: > **~ja** (e.g., *dewa mata* -> "ja mata").
content.usage:
Rule: ~de wa
Description: > **~ja** (e.g., *dewa mata* -> "ja mata").

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::~te iru}} is defined as: > **~teru** (e.g., *shitte iru* -> "shitteru").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de '~te iru' es: > **~teru** (e.g., *shitte iru* -> "shitteru").
content.usage:
Rule: ~te iru
Description: > **~teru** (e.g., *shitte iru* -> "shitteru").

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::ai}} is defined as: > **ee** (e.g., *itai* -> "itee" [iteː], *wakaranai* -> "wakarane").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'ai' es: > **ee** (e.g., *itai* -> "itee" [iteː], *wakaranai* -> "wakarane").
content.usage:
Rule: ai
Description: > **ee** (e.g., *itai* -> "itee" [iteː], *wakaranai* -> "wakarane").

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::oi}} is defined as: > **ee** (e.g., *sugoi* -> "sugee" [sɯɡeː], *osoi* -> "osee").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'oi' es: > **ee** (e.g., *sugoi* -> "sugee" [sɯɡeː], *osoi* -> "osee").
content.usage:
Rule: oi
Description: > **ee** (e.g., *sugoi* -> "sugee" [sɯɡeː], *osoi* -> "osee").

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::ui}} is defined as: > **ii** (e.g., *warui* -> "warii").
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'ui' es: > **ii** (e.g., *warui* -> "warii").
content.usage:
Rule: ui
Description: > **ii** (e.g., *warui* -> "warii").

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pitch Accent}} is defined as: Unlike English stress accent, Japanese uses changes in pitch (High vs. Low).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Pitch Accent' es: Unlike English stress accent, Japanese uses changes in pitch (High vs. Low).
content.usage:
Rule: Pitch Accent
Description: Unlike English stress accent, Japanese uses changes in pitch (High vs. Low).

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Accent Patterns}} is defined as: - **Heiban** (Flat): Pitch rises and stays high.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Japanese.
content.spanish: En la fonética de Japanese, la regla de 'Accent Patterns' es: - **Heiban** (Flat): Pitch rises and stays high.
content.usage:
Rule: Accent Patterns
Description: - **Heiban** (Flat): Pitch rises and stays high.

content.text: What is the Japanese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Desu" Whisper}}? When pronouncing "desu" or "masu", do not voice the final "u". Whisper it, or omit it completely: say "dess" and "mass".
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Japanese.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Japanese para 'The "Desu" Whisper'? When pronouncing "desu" or "masu", do not voice the final "u". Whisper it, or omit it completely: say "dess" and "mass".
content.usage:
Tip: The "Desu" Whisper
When pronouncing "desu" or "masu", do not voice the final "u". Whisper it, or omit it completely: say "dess" and "mass".

content.text: What is the Japanese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Everyday Contraction Rule}}? If you are speaking casually, never use full form "te iru" or "te shimau". Use "shitteru" instead of "shitte iru" and "tabechau" instead of "tabete shimau" to sound completely native.
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Japanese.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Japanese para 'Everyday Contraction Rule'? If you are speaking casually, never use full form "te iru" or "te shimau". Use "shitteru" instead of "shitte iru" and "tabechau" instead of "tabete shimau" to sound completely native.
content.usage:
Tip: Everyday Contraction Rule
If you are speaking casually, never use full form "te iru" or "te shimau". Use "shitteru" instead of "shitte iru" and "tabechau" instead of "tabete shimau" to sound completely native.

content.text: What is the Japanese pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? Think of Japanese as a drum beat; every character (mora) gets one beat of time.
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for Japanese.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Japanese para 'consejo'? Think of Japanese as a drum beat; every character (mora) gets one beat of time.
content.usage:
Tip: Tip
Think of Japanese as a drum beat; every character (mora) gets one beat of time.

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Devoicing (無声化)}} is defined as: High vowels /i/ and /u/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants or at the end of a sentence following a voiceless consonant (e.g., *desu* → "des'", *suki* → "s-ki").
content.explanation: Devoicing occurs because the vocal cords do not vibrate during the transition between two voiceless consonants (like /s/ and /k/), silencing the high vowels.
content.spanish: Dessonorización de Vocales 'U' e 'I': Las vocales altas pierden su sonoridad cuando están entre consonantes sordas o al final de frase tras consonante sorda. Ejemplo: "desu" → "des'".
content.usage:
Rule: Vowel Devoicing (無声化)
Examples:
  • desu (to be) → "des'"
  • suki (like) → "s-ki"
  • shita (under) → "sh-ta"

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Sokuon (促音)}} is defined as: The small *tsu* (っ) represents a double consonant, causing a one-mora pause (air blockage) before pronouncing the next sorda consonant (e.g., *matte* → [maʔte]).
content.explanation: The Sokuon represents a geminate consonant that takes exactly one mora (unit of time) of silence, during which the mouth prepares the shape for the next consonant.
content.spanish: Sokuon (っ): La pequeña tsu representa una pausa de una mora (detención de aire) antes de una consonante sorda. Ejemplo: "matte" → "ma-tte".
content.usage:
Rule: Sokuon (促音)
Examples:
  • matte (wait) → "ma [pause] te"
  • gakkou (school) → "ga [pause] kou"

content.text: In Japanese phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Colloquial Contractions}} is defined as: High-frequency verb endings compress in casual fast speech (e.g., *~te shimau* → "~tchau", *~de wa* → "~ja").
content.explanation: Common grammaticized auxiliaries contract heavily in casual speed: the glide /j/ fuses with preceding dental consonants.
content.spanish: Contracciones Coloquiales: Reducciones extremas de terminaciones de verbos en el habla rápida informal. Ejemplo: "~te shimau" → "~tchau".
content.usage:
Rule: Colloquial Contractions
Examples:
  • tabete shimau → "tabetchau"
  • de wa nai → "ja nai"
  • te oku → "toku"

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "好きです (Suki desu)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::S-ki des'.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Vowel Devoicing
High vowels /u/ and /i/ lose their voicing between voiceless consonants /s/, /k/, /t/.
content.spanish: Spanish: Me gusta / Es así.
content.usage: Formal: 好きです (Suki desu). → Connected: S-ki des'.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "ちょっと待ってください (Chotto matte kudasai)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Chotto ma[pause]te kudasai.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Sokuon (促音)
The small tsu represents a one-mora silent pause, holding the breath before the /t/ sound.
content.spanish: Spanish: Espere un momento, por favor.
content.usage: Formal: ちょっと待ってください (Chotto matte kudasai). → Connected: Chotto ma[pause]te kudasai.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "食べてしまった (Tabete shimatta)."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Tabetchatta.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Colloquial Contractions
The auxiliary verb ending '-te shimatta' fuses and contracts into '-tchatta' in casual speech.
content.spanish: Spanish: Terminé comiéndolo.
content.usage: Formal: 食べてしまった (Tabete shimatta). → Connected: Tabetchatta.
